does: This paper proposes ClaHF, a reinforcement learning framework that improves text classification by moving beyond traditional supervised fine-tuning. Rather than treating each training instance independently, ClaHF generates multiple candidate predictions for each input and learns to rank them according to their quality, encoding this preference information into a reward model. The reward model jointly captures both which prediction is best and how non-optimal predictions rank relative to each other. This preference signal is then used to optimize the classification policy through reinforcement learning. The framework requires no additional human annotations beyond the original labels, instead converting standard instance-wise supervision into relative preference relations that better guide the model's decision boundaries and confidence calibration.
connects: This work builds on the recent surge of preference-based learning approaches in large language models, particularly drawing from techniques like RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) that have proven successful in instruction following and generation tasks. It extends preference modeling beyond pairwise comparisons to handle ranking information among multiple candidates, similar to advances in learning-to-rank methods. The paper responds to limitations in supervised fine-tuning identified in the broader literature on calibration and confidence estimation in neural classifiers, while also connecting to work on implicit reward signals and self-generated supervision where models learn from their own predictions rather than requiring explicit human judgments.
enables: ClaHF opens pathways for improving classification without costly annotation efforts, suggesting that preference-based optimization could be more broadly applied across NLP tasks beyond generation. The multi-candidate ranking approach could inspire future work on richer supervision signals that capture nuanced quality distinctions. The framework potentially enables better-calibrated classifiers with improved decision boundaries, which could particularly benefit applications requiring high confidence accuracy tradeoffs. Future work might extend this to few-shot or zero-shot classification settings, explore active learning strategies to select which candidates to rank, or apply similar preference-based RL frameworks to structured prediction tasks where multiple valid outputs exist per input.
